verdict: Sample-more only helps if you select by CONFIDENCE, not by majority -- and the confidence signal also tells you when sampling is futile. (1) Two regimes: familiar_induce is COVERAGE-limited (greedy 0.212 -> pass@12 0.9), novel_induce CAPABILITY-limited (greedy 0.075 -> pass@12 0.588, below pure-luck). (2) VERIFICATION-FREE SELECTION: picking the highest per-sample P(answer) gets 0.617 vs self-consistency/majority 0.479 vs random 0.438 (oracle 0.829) -- and across budgets self-consistency is FLAT (~0.48) while confidence-select RISES to 0.62. It works because when the model derives the right rule it is confident, so the most-confident sample beats the most-common one (C40: P(answer) is calibrated). (3) ABSTENTION: max per-sample P(answer) predicts solvability at AUROC 0.83; abstaining on low-confidence gives ~1.0 accuracy on the confident top third. (Confidence-guided ALLOCATION is ~tied with uniform confidence-select -- selection, not allocation, is where the win is.)
